data.title: Rivian’s Profit Milestone Reframes Risks As R2 SUV And VW Deal Near
data.description:
• Rivian Automotive reported a positive gross profit in the fourth quarter, marking a key shift in its unit economics.
  • The company is preparing for the launch of its R2 midsized SUV in 2026, targeting a broader mass market audience.
  • Rivian advanced its technology joint venture with Volkswagen Group, triggering new funding phases tied to software and platform development.

Rivian Automotive, NasdaqGS:RIVN, is drawing fresh attention after reaching positive gross profit in the fourth quarter, a change that directly addresses long standing questions about its path to profitability. The stock closed at $16.47, with a 7 day return of 11.6% and a 1 year return of 18.2%, while year to date performance is a 15.1% decline and the 30 day return is a 1.2% decline. This underscores how sentiment around the name has been mixed. Against that backdrop, improving unit economics give investors new data points to weigh alongside the share price moves.

The planned 2026 launch of the R2 SUV and the progress on the Volkswagen joint venture signal that Rivian is working to scale both its product lineup and its technology platform. For you as an investor, the combination of a mass market model and a software centric partnership could be important in assessing how the company might grow its addressable market and potentially spread fixed costs over a larger base over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Rivian Automotive by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Rivian Automotive.

We've flagged 3 risks for Rivian Automotive. See which could impact your investment.

For you as an investor, the key takeaway is that Rivian is starting to show that its business model can generate gross profit while it prepares to push into a much larger market with the R2 midsized SUV. The fourth quarter still came with a sizeable net loss of US$811 million and full year losses of US$3.65b, so this is not yet a profit story. However, positive gross profit, strong growth in software and services revenue, and progress on the Volkswagen technology joint venture together point to a mix of hardware and software that could support more diversified revenue over time. The planned R2 launch in 2026 targets a segment currently led by names like Tesla’s Model Y, Ford’s Mustang Mach E and Hyundai’s Ioniq 5, which sets up direct competition on price, range and features. At the same time, Rivian’s liquidity, including cash, joint venture funding and access to a Department of Energy loan, may give it room to keep investing in manufacturing and its autonomy platform while it works through losses.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Rivian Automotive Narrative

  • Positive gross profit, R2 preparation and the Volkswagen joint venture all align with the narrative that a more efficient R2 platform and technology partnerships could improve Rivian’s cost structure and broaden its revenue sources.
  • The continued multi billion dollar annual net loss and reliance on large capital commitments keep the cash burn and execution risk highlighted in the narrative firmly in focus, especially with so much riding on a successful R2 rollout.
  • The stronger contribution from software and services revenue, including joint venture related income, may not be fully reflected in earlier narrative assumptions that focused more heavily on vehicle volumes.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Rivian Automotive to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Rivian is still reporting large net losses, with US$811 million in the fourth quarter and US$3.65b for 2025, so the path to company wide profitability remains uncertain.
  • ⚠️ The R2 launch and Volkswagen joint venture need to execute well in a competitive EV market that includes Tesla, Ford and others, and any setback in volumes, pricing or software deployment could pressure the story.
  • 🎁 Achieving positive gross profit alongside strong software and services growth suggests Rivian’s unit economics and technology platform are moving in a direction that could support better margins over time.
  • 🎁 The combination of the R2 mass market SUV, Volkswagen funding commitments and Department of Energy loan access provides Rivian with multiple potential revenue channels and funding sources as it scales.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track whether Rivian can sustain positive gross profit while preparing for the R2 ramp and whether software and services continue to represent a larger share of revenue. The Volkswagen joint venture milestones, including any new capital tranches and product launches that use Rivian’s software stack, will be important signals of commercial traction. On the product side, watch for R2 reservation trends, pricing details and any commentary on how it is positioned against SUVs from Tesla, Ford and Hyundai. Finally, monitor updates around cash usage, capital raising plans and guidance for deliveries, since these will shape how long Rivian can fund its expansion on current resources.

data.title: Fletcher Building (NZSE:FBU) Wider H2 Loss Tests Earnings Recovery Narrative
data.description:
Fletcher Building (NZSE:FBU) has just posted its H1 2026 numbers, with revenue of NZ$3.4b and a basic EPS loss of NZ$0.26, while trailing twelve month figures show revenue of NZ$7.0b and a basic EPS loss of NZ$0.22. The company has seen revenue move from NZ$3.8b in H2 2024 to NZ$3.6b in H1 2025 and NZ$3.4b in H2 2025, alongside net income losses of NZ$72m, NZ$82m and NZ$285m over those halves. For investors, the headline story is still about pressured margins and loss making operations, which sets up this result as a test of the longer term earnings recovery narrative.

See our full analysis for Fletcher Building.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to weigh this earnings print against the big narratives around Fletcher Building, and see which stories hold up and which start to look stretched.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Losses Widen To NZ$285m On Lower H2 Revenue

  • H2 2025 revenue was NZ$3,411 million with a net loss (excluding extra items) of NZ$285 million, compared with losses of NZ$82 million and NZ$72 million on NZ$3,583 million and NZ$3,823 million of revenue in the prior two halves.
  • Critics highlight the bearish point that Fletcher Building is still unprofitable, and the trailing 12 month loss of NZ$234 million alongside a five year loss growth rate of about 46.8% a year supports that concern.
    • Across the last three reported halves, net losses of NZ$72 million, NZ$82 million and NZ$285 million sit against revenue between NZ$3,411 million and NZ$3,823 million, so higher revenue in past periods has not translated into profits.
    • The trailing 12 month basic EPS loss of NZ$0.22 and repeated negative net income figures back the view that margin pressure remains a central risk rather than a short term blip.

TTM Revenue Around NZ$7.0b With Ongoing EPS Loss

  • On a trailing 12 month basis to H1 2026, Fletcher Building generated NZ$7.0b of revenue and recorded a basic EPS loss of NZ$0.22, with net income excluding extra items at a loss of NZ$234 million and NZ$62 million of losses from discontinued operations.
  • Supporters of a recovery story point out that earnings are forecast to grow by about 47.96% a year and to become profitable within three years, and the current EPS loss and TTM revenue base give that bullish view a concrete starting point.
    • Forecast revenue growth of 2.4% a year sits below the 4% NZ market benchmark, so any bullish case is leaning more on a turnaround in profitability than on rapid top line expansion from the current NZ$7.0b level.
    • The fact that TTM revenue has been near NZ$7.0b in recent periods while losses remain suggests that a future earnings recovery would need either cost improvement or mix changes rather than just more sales.

Some investors want to see how this earnings path lines up with different long term storylines for the company, and that is where the broader narrative work can help connect these raw numbers to possible future scenarios. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Fletcher Building.

Cheap 0.5x P/S Versus Peers And Cited DCF Value

  • At a share price of NZ$3.51, Fletcher Building trades on a P/S of 0.5x compared with 1.1x for the global building industry and 2.5x for peers, and against a cited DCF fair value of about NZ$8.27 per share, which implies the market price sits roughly 57.6% below that modelled value.
  • What stands out for bullish investors is the tension between this low 0.5x P/S multiple and the company’s loss making record.
    • The combination of a trailing 12 month net loss of NZ$234 million and forecasts for earnings to become profitable within three years is being compared directly against the gap between NZ$3.51 and the NZ$8.27 DCF fair value.
    • Backers of the bullish view see the discounted multiple and the cited upside to DCF fair value as reasons to watch for an earnings turn, while the history of widening losses over five years keeps that view firmly tied to execution on profit improvement rather than valuation alone.

data.title: Synopsys Bets On AI And Ecosystem Strength With SimuTech Recognition
data.description:
• Synopsys recognized SimuTech Group as its 2025 Worldwide Channel Partner of the Year, highlighting performance across sales, support, and customer enablement.
  • The company is expanding AI driven electronic design automation tools aimed at streamlining chip and system design workflows.
  • Recent partnerships, including NVIDIA's investment and deeper integration with Ansys, reflect collaboration across hardware, software, and simulation.

Synopsys (NasdaqGS:SNPS) operates at the center of chip design software, and the latest partner award underscores its reliance on a strong ecosystem to support customers globally. The shares last closed at $421.95, with a 3 year return of 19.8% and a 5 year return of 66.6%. Over the past year the stock has declined 19.6%, including a 12.2% decline year to date and an 18.3% decline over the past month.

For investors, the focus here is less on short term price moves and more on how AI infused design tools and high profile alliances may shape Synopsys' role in chip development over time. The combination of recognized partners, AI driven EDA offerings, and cooperation with large industry players such as NVIDIA and Ansys points to positioning that extends beyond any single earnings report.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Synopsys by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Synopsys.

We've flagged 2 risks for Synopsys. See which could impact your investment.

This partner award and the broader set of alliances point to Synopsys leaning harder into an ecosystem model, where its AI-powered EDA tools, Ansys simulation, and partners like SimuTech, NVIDIA, and chip customers reinforce each other. For you as an investor, the key question is whether this network can keep Synopsys central to complex chip and system design as workflows evolve. NVIDIA’s US$2.0b stake and joint work on accelerated computing and digital twins, plus customer collaborations with companies such as Lightmatter and Edgewater Wireless, suggest Synopsys is trying to embed its tools deeper into next generation AI and connectivity projects. At the same time, the stock has recently been weak and the IP business and China exposure are under pressure, so partnership news sits alongside real execution questions. Against peers such as Cadence Design Systems and Siemens EDA, this push into AI-driven, chiplet friendly design flows and system level simulation looks aimed at keeping Synopsys on short lists for leading edge programs rather than competing only on point tools.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Synopsys Narrative

  • The focus on AI-powered design, chiplet workflows, and deeper Ansys integration supports the narrative that Synopsys is moving from pure EDA toward broader silicon to systems engineering, which could help it serve AI, automotive, and industrial customers more fully.
  • Concerns in the narrative about the IP segment and China headwinds are a counterweight, as capacity shifts toward AI and data center chips may not automatically translate into higher monetization for the US$1.75b IP business without careful execution.
  • The growing role of channel partners like SimuTech and collaborations with customers building photonics, Wi Fi 8, and automotive AI SoCs add an ecosystem angle that is not fully captured in the existing focus on IP mix, margins, and Ansys cost synergies.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Synopsys to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk around integrating Ansys, scaling AI-driven tools, and managing a shift in engineering capacity toward AI and data center projects while the IP segment and China revenue face pressure.
  • ⚠️ Competitive pressure from other EDA vendors such as Cadence and Siemens EDA, which are also investing in AI-assisted design flows and could compete for the same high value chip projects.
  • 🎁 The recognition of SimuTech as Worldwide Channel Partner of the Year, plus NVIDIA’s US$2.0b investment and customer collaborations, suggests Synopsys is building relationships that can support demand for its tools and services across multiple end markets.
  • 🎁 Analysts have identified rewards including earnings growth expectations and views that the stock trades below some price targets. Combined with AI and high performance computing demand, this frames a potential upside case if the partnerships translate into sustained revenue and profit growth.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how quickly Synopsys turns these partnerships into visible contract wins, backlog growth, and stable margins, especially in the Design IP segment. Earnings on 25 February 2026 will be one checkpoint for updated guidance and commentary on China, AI-driven demand, and Ansys integration. It is also worth tracking whether customers in AI, automotive, and cloud keep standardizing on Synopsys flows over alternatives from Cadence and Siemens, and whether management gives more concrete targets for cost synergies and AI-related productivity gains.

data.description:

Alongside this, ongoing coverage of the company has kept attention on its role in data services and AI related work, giving context to why sentiment may have shifted at different points.

Innodata scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Innodata Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value using a required rate of return.

For Innodata, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is US$39.24 million. Analysts provide an estimate of US$27.35 million for 2026, and Simply Wall St extends this with ten year projections, which gradually step down from US$27.35 million in 2026 to US$18.61 million by 2035, with each year discounted back to today.

When all those discounted cash flows are added up, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$12.52 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$43.02, this implies Innodata is around 243.6% above the DCF estimate. On this model the stock screens as significantly overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Innodata may be overvalued by 243.6%. Discover 56 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Innodata.

Approach 2: Innodata Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It quickly connects the share price to what the business is currently generating in profit, which is often a primary driver of long term returns.

What counts as a “normal” P/E will vary. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower one. So you want to compare a company’s P/E to relevant benchmarks rather than in isolation.

Innodata currently trades on a P/E of 40.74x. That sits above both the Professional Services industry average of 19.43x and the peer average of 20.96x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Innodata of 27.35x, which is the P/E they would expect given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for the company’s own profile instead of assuming it should match the average. Set against the actual P/E of 40.74x, the Fair Ratio suggests Innodata’s shares are pricing in a higher level of expectations than this framework implies.

Result: OVERVALUED

Here the numbers give them some support because the 10.5% multi year growth rate sits beside that 0.2% latest year figure, while half year EPS figures over 2024 and 2025 move in a fairly tight band between A$0.70 and A$0.77, which suggests recent progress has been modest even with high earnings quality flagged in the analysis.
    • Critics can point to H1 2025 net income of A$20.3 million versus H2 2025 net income of A$18.5 million as another example of how earnings have moved around a relatively narrow range instead of clearly accelerating.
    • At the same time, the fact that earnings quality is described as high means the slower 0.2% growth is not being cushioned by any mention of weaker accounting quality in the trailing data.

P/E of 24.1x with price far above DCF fair value

  • On valuation, the shares trade on a 24.1x P/E, which is lower than the peer average of 32.7x but higher than the wider Australian Capital Markets industry at 20.9x. The current A$36 share price sits well above the DCF fair value of about A$7.44.
  • What might surprise bullish investors who anchor on the P/E discount to peers is that the same data set flags the A$36 price as being far higher than the A$7.44 DCF fair value and also notes an unstable dividend record. Anyone leaning on the relative P/E alone has to weigh that against a valuation model that implies a very large premium to modelled cash flows and a dividend history that is not described as steady for income focused holders.
    • Supporters of the bullish case can reasonably highlight the five year 10.5% earnings growth and high earnings quality, yet the gap between A$36 and A$7.44 suggests the market is paying a lot compared with that specific cash flow estimate.
    • Income oriented investors also have to factor in that, alongside the P/E and DCF signals, the dividend track record is described as unstable rather than consistently growing or maintained.

data.title: CervoMed (NASDAQ:CRVO) Will Have To Spend Its Cash Wisely
data.description:
Even when a business is losing money, it's possible for shareholders to make money if they buy a good business at the right price. Indeed, CervoMed (NASDAQ:CRVO) stock is up 104% in the last year, providing strong gains for shareholders. Having said that, unprofitable companies are risky because they could potentially burn through all their cash and become distressed.

Given its strong share price performance, we think it's worthwhile for CervoMed shareholders to consider whether its cash burn is concerning. In this article, we define cash burn as its annual (negative) free cash flow, which is the amount of money a company spends each year to fund its growth. The first step is to compare its cash burn with its cash reserves, to give us its 'cash runway'.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

How Long Is CervoMed's Cash Runway?

A cash runway is defined as the length of time it would take a company to run out of money if it kept spending at its current rate of cash burn. In September 2025, CervoMed had US$27m in cash, and was debt-free. Importantly, its cash burn was US$25m over the trailing twelve months. Therefore, from September 2025 it had roughly 13 months of cash runway. That's not too bad, but it's fair to say the end of the cash runway is in sight, unless cash burn reduces drastically. The image below shows how its cash balance has been changing over the last few years.

See our latest analysis for CervoMed

How Well Is CervoMed Growing?

Notably, CervoMed actually ramped up its cash burn very hard and fast in the last year, by 126%, signifying heavy investment in the business. While that's concerning on it's own, the fact that operating revenue was actually down 39% over the same period makes us positively tremulous. Considering these two factors together makes us nervous about the direction the company seems to be heading. While the past is always worth studying, it is the future that matters most of all. For that reason, it makes a lot of sense to take a look at our analyst forecasts for the company.

Can CervoMed Raise More Cash Easily?

Since CervoMed can't yet boast improving growth metrics, the market will likely be considering how it can raise more cash if need be. Generally speaking, a listed business can raise new cash through issuing shares or taking on debt. One of the main advantages held by publicly listed companies is that they can sell shares to investors to raise cash and fund growth. We can compare a company's cash burn to its market capitalisation to get a sense for how many new shares a company would have to issue to fund one year's operations.

CervoMed has a market capitalisation of US$44m and burnt through US$25m last year, which is 57% of the company's market value. That's high expenditure relative to the value of the entire company, so if it does have to issue shares to fund more growth, that could end up really hurting shareholders returns (through significant dilution).

Is CervoMed's Cash Burn A Worry?

On this analysis of CervoMed's cash burn, we think its cash runway was reassuring, while its increasing cash burn has us a bit worried. After looking at that range of measures, we think shareholders should be extremely attentive to how the company is using its cash, as the cash burn makes us uncomfortable.
